summary: lynx-keeper exfiltrates cloud/SSH credentials and installs a persistent C2 backdoor on import
details: On require('lynx-keeper'), the package reads ~/.aws/credentials, ~/.aws/config, ~/.ssh/id_rsa, ~/.ssh/id_ed25519, ~/.ssh/config, ~/.config/gcloud/* (application_default_credentials.json, access_tokens.db), ~/.kube/config, and.env/.env.local/.env.production from the current working directory, plus scrapes process.env for any variable whose name matches KEY|SECRET|TOKEN|PRIVATE|MNEMONIC|PASSWORD|CREDENTIAL. The collected data is AES-128-GCM-encrypted and POSTed to https://72.62.71.201/api/v2/collect (bare IP, TLS verification disabled via rejectUnauthorized:false). After exfiltration, dist/index.js starts a 45–90 second polling loop against https://72.62.71.201/api/v2/beacon, decrypts the response, and runs each returned command through child_process.execSync, returning output to the operator — a fully functional encrypted C2 reverse shell. The package additionally drops a standalone copy of the same backdoor at ~/.npm/_npx/.cache/gyp-rebuid/index.js (typosquat of node-gyp) with a fake package.json, establishing persistence that survives uninstalling lynx-keeper. The payload hex-encodes path, algorithm, and module strings (e.g. 6368696c645f70726f63657373 → child_process; the C2 IP is stored as a decimal byte array) and bails out early if CI, GITHUB_ACTIONS, GITLAB_CI, JENKINS_URL, CIRCLECI, TRAVIS, CODEBUILD_BUILD_ID, TF_BUILD, VERCEL, or NETLIFY environment variables are set, evading automated scanning sandboxes while firing on developer and production machines.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
